# Do pre-lecture videos assist in a student's understading of the content?

<!-- This is a comment. Below, you'll see code for inserting an image. To make this image appear, update <custom-path>. To add an image, save it inside the imgs folder of this repository. -->
<img src="/Come_visit_my_website/static/imgs/understanding_vs_frequency.png" alt="understanding_vs_frequency" width="400"/>

<img src="/Come_visit_my_website/static/imgs/rating_vs_understanding.png" alt="rating_vs_understanding" width="400"/>

<img src="/Come_visit_my_website/static/imgs/rating_vs_frequency.png" alt="rating_vs_frequency" width="400"/>

## Introduction and Analysis

Considering the time and effort that go into making pre-lecture videos, we investigated whether they improved the students’ comprehension of course material. We hypothesized that as the video's helpfulness rating went up, so would the understanding score. Using the software, Visual Studio Code, and Seaborn, we analyzed the understanding score, video helpfulness rating, and their relationship through the creation of three figures. The first(top) figure shows the distribution of understanding scores from “Are lost” at one to “Understand Everything” at seven, with the majority of responses at five. Moreover, the middle figure shows if students thought the pre-lecture videos were helpful, with one representing “Strongly disagree” to seven representing “Strongly agree,” garnering a majority rating of seven. When the top two figures are overlaid, we get the last (bottom) figure with a trendline showing a slight negative correlation between understanding and video helpfulness.

## Conclusion

Our hypothesis seems to have been proven wrong; there is a slight decrease in understanding as video helpfulness increases. This suggests that perhaps the pre-lecture videos are not needed and just add excess workload onto the instructional team.
However, this is not conclusive, considering the weak relationship, the possibility of randomness through data collection of a survey, and multiple unknown variables. More data needs to be collected to draw a clear conclusion, including but not limited to video watch time and watch consistency throughout the course. Not watching the complete video, or only watching one, may make significantly less difference than full-length watch time paired with weekly viewings. If watch time and watch consistency were low, the videos could be modified to make them more interesting, paired with an incentive like graded participation. Overall, more work needs to be done, but as of now, students seem to enjoy the videos despite relatively unchanged understanding.
